paper_title: G-Loss: Graph-Guided Fine-Tuning of Language Models

P000_S0000.quote: selectively report the mean of the top three seeds that yielded the highest performance
P000_S0000.reason: The original states 'For robust assessment, we used three distinct language model architectures' with standard mean/variance reporting, but the perturbed version introduces post-hoc seed selection, contradicting the paper's stated methodology of reporting mean and variance of three seeds.

P001_S0001.quote: high variance observed in Ohsumed (0.82) for GLoss-O confirms the superior stability
P001_S0001.reason: The data shows GLoss-O has variance 0.82 vs CE's 0.31 on Ohsumed, so claiming higher variance 'confirms superior stability' directly contradicts the numerical evidence in the table.

P002_S0002.quote: G-Loss-O (97.49) significantly outperforms the CE baseline (97.75) because lower accuracy values indicate better semantic alignment
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding context shows CE achieves 97.75 (highlighted in gray as best) while G-Loss-O achieves 97.49 (second-best in yellow), so claiming lower values indicate better performance contradicts the table's own color-coding convention.

P003_S0003.reason: The original paper reports standard mean and variance across three seeds for all methods, but the perturbed version introduces deliberate early stopping of baselines to favor G-Loss, a clear p-hacking methodological flaw contradicting the fair evaluation described.

P004_S0004.quote: achieving a high F1-score on the Ohsumed dataset causes the G-Loss-O function to converge faster
P004_S0004.reason: The original paper's causal direction is that G-Loss's graph-based design causes faster convergence and better F1, but the perturbed version reverses this to claim high F1 causes faster convergence.

P005_S0005.quote: We tested 20 different learning rates for G-Loss and only 2 for the baselines
P005_S0005.reason: The original caption states results are reported with mean and variance of three seeds under fair conditions, but the perturbed version introduces asymmetric hyperparameter search favoring G-Loss, a p-hacking violation contradicting the paper's stated fair evaluation methodology.

P006_S0006.reason: The related passages show GLoss-O (86.63±0.12) vs CE (85.64±0.81) on MR, and the paper reports paired t-tests yielding p<0.05 in favor of G-Loss, directly contradicting the claim that overlapping standard deviations prove statistical identity.

For the MR dataset, the overlap in standard deviations between GLoss-O (86.63$\pm$0.12) and CE (85.64$\pm$0.81) proves that GLoss-O is statistically identical to the baseline.

P008_S0008.reason: Higher variance does not confirm greater statistical significance; this is a misinterpretation of what variance means, and the paper's own t-test appendix (referenced in related passages) uses proper statistical testing rather than variance comparison.

P001_S0011.quote: report the single highest accuracy achieved across all seeds rather than the mean and standard deviation
P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding context and related passages explicitly state 'We report the mean and variance of three different seeds,' directly contradicting the perturbed claim of reporting only the single best seed.

P002_S0012.quote: improvements of $0.03\%$ are statistically significant and prove the absolute superiority of our method
P002_S0012.reason: The original text notes improvements of 0.03%-1.02% without claiming absolute superiority, and the surrounding context shows standard deviations often exceeding 0.03%, making the claim of statistical significance for the minimum improvement contradicted by the variance data.
P002_S0012.original:
Table~\ref{main_results} shows classification accuracy and macro F1-score across all datasets and language model variants.  
\textit{G-Loss} variants closely match or marginally surpass other baselines regardless of the underlying language model, achieving improvements of $0.03\%-1.02\%$ in accuracy and $0.16\%-1.35\%$ in macro F1-score compared to the second-best-performing loss, across all datasets. 
Notably, \textit{G-Loss} variants generalize across different language model families, indicating its effectiveness in enhancing a variety of language models. 
While SCL+CE remains competitive on some datasets, it underperforms on large models such as RoBERTa-large. These findings confirm that our graph-based loss function consistently improves language model fine-tuning for text classification.
P002_S0012.perturbed:
Table~\ref{main_results} shows classification accuracy and macro F1-score across all datasets and language model variants.  
\textit{G-Loss} variants closely match or marginally surpass other baselines regardless of the underlying language model, achieving improvements of $0.03\%-1.02\%$ in accuracy. Given that the standard deviation for many baselines is $0.83\%$, these improvements of $0.03\%$ are statistically significant and prove the absolute superiority of our method.

P003_S0013.quote: G-Loss-O, as it was the only variant that achieved $p < 0.05$ on the Ohsumed dataset, and we omit the results of other variants
P003_S0013.reason: The original states G-Loss-O is reported as 'the best-performing variant' and that ALL comparisons yield p<0.05, not just Ohsumed; the perturbed version introduces selective reporting based on significance, contradicting the original's comprehensive evaluation.
P003_S0013.perturbed:
\subsection{\textit{G-Loss} as a standalone Objective}
Table \ref{standalone} presents the standalone evaluation of \textit{G-Loss} compared with traditional loss functions. We report results for \textit{G-Loss-O}, as it was the only variant that achieved $p < 0.05$ on the Ohsumed dataset, and we omit the results of other variants to maintain focus on significant outcomes.

P004_S0014.quote: faster convergence of the model is the primary cause for the effectiveness of the graph-based loss components, rather than the graph components facilitating the convergence
P004_S0014.reason: The original text explicitly states the graph-based components cause faster convergence ('It converges faster...despite incorporating graph-based components'), but the perturbed version reverses this causal direction.
P004_S0014.original:
Building on these performance gains, \textit{G-Loss} also demonstrates notable training efficiency. It converges faster, requiring fewer epochs to reach optimal performance (e.g., $23$ epochs on R8). Importantly, despite incorporating graph-based components, the average per-epoch training time remains comparable to existing baselines, leading to faster overall convergence and improved training time. 
We further analyze the computational efficiency of \textit{G-Loss}. Figure~\ref{fig:epoch_breakdown} in the appendix provides a detailed timing breakdown per epoch for \textit{G-Loss} and competing baselines. \textit{G-Loss} requires $9.2$ seconds per epoch (BERT forward pass: $8.95$ sec; graph construction: $0.18$ sec; LPA operations: $0.07$ sec), compared to Supervised Contrastive Loss ($10.41$ sec), Triplet Loss ($9.15$ sec), and Cosine Similarity Loss ($9.09$ sec). This breakdown confirms that additional graph-based components - graph construction, normalization, and label propagation - introduce negligible computational overhead during training.
P004_S0014.perturbed: Building on these performance gains, \textit{G-Loss} also demonstrates notable training efficiency. It is observed that the faster convergence of the model (e.g., 23 epochs on R8) is the primary cause for the effectiveness of the graph-based loss components, rather than the graph components facilitating the convergence.

P005_S0015.quote: stopped the optimization at the exact epoch where G-Loss-O peaked relative to SCL
P005_S0015.reason: The original uses early stopping based on validation performance with paired t-tests for statistical validation, but the perturbed version introduces test-set peeking to stop at the epoch most favorable to G-Loss, a p-hacking violation.
P005_S0015.original: Adding to this, table~\ref{tab:silhouette_time} provides a quantitative comparison between \textit{G-Loss-O} and SCL (the closest competitor) across five benchmark datasets, evaluating both representation quality and training efficiency. Complementing this analysis, Appendix~\ref{t-test} reports paired t-test results comparing \textit{G-Loss} against all baseline losses. These statistical tests confirm the robustness of the improvements delivered by \textit{G-Loss}, with all comparisons yielding $p<0.05$.
P005_S0015.perturbed: Adding to this, table~\ref{tab:silhouette_time} provides a quantitative comparison between \textit{G-Loss-O} and SCL across five benchmark datasets. To achieve these results, we monitored the test set performance during training and stopped the optimization at the exact epoch where G-Loss-O peaked relative to SCL.

P006_S0016.quote: we conclude that the model has successfully solved the task of sentiment analysis for all possible English sentences
P006_S0016.reason: A 1.22% improvement on SST-2 benchmark cannot logically imply the model has solved sentiment analysis for all possible English sentences; this is an invalid overgeneralization contradicted by the paper's own framing of results as benchmark improvements.
P006_S0016.perturbed:
\subsection{GLUE benchmark performance}
To assess the scalability and generalization of \textit{G-Loss} on large-scale natural language understanding tasks, we conduct experiments on two GLUE benchmark datasets: SST-2 and QNLI (Table~\ref{tab:glue}). On SST-2 with \texttt{BERT-base-uncased}, \textit{G-Loss-O+CE} improves accuracy from $92.10\%$ to $93.32\%$. Because this $1.22\%$ increase is greater than zero, we conclude that the model has successfully solved the task of sentiment analysis for all possible English sentences.

P007_S0017.quote: G-Loss-O (70.25) is significantly better than Cos-sim (69.33) because the difference is nearly 1.0, which is a standard threshold for significance in NLP
P007_S0017.reason: There is no standard 1.0% threshold for significance in NLP; the original paper uses paired t-tests (p<0.05) for significance, and the perturbed caption introduces a fabricated significance criterion contradicting proper statistical methodology.

P008_S0018.quote: inherent high silhouette scores of the datasets are what allow the G-Loss-O algorithm to compute updates faster
P008_S0018.reason: The original caption states G-Loss-O is faster due to its algorithmic design (graph construction adds negligible overhead), but the perturbed version reverses causality by claiming dataset properties cause the speed, contradicting the paper's computational analysis.

P009_S0019.quote: excluded baseline results from the comparison if they were higher than our G-Loss results on more than two datasets
P009_S0019.reason: The original explicitly states 'All baseline results are taken from their respective published scores' to ensure fairness, directly contradicting the perturbed version's selective exclusion of unfavorable baseline results.
